content.text: Quality and reliability are related but distinct properties of a Claude integration. Quality measures whether the integration produces the right outputs — whether the model's responses are accurate, safe, and aligned with the intended behaviour. Reliability measures whether the integration serves users consistently — whether it is available, fast enough, and error-free. A team that tracks only quality can ship a correct integration that is frequently unavailable. A team that tracks only reliability can ship an integration that is always up but silently producing wrong or unsafe outputs. Both scorecards are necessary.

attrs.alt: Diagram: Quality Scorecard (correctness metrics) vs Reliability Scorecard (stability metrics) — their distinct focus and primary signals

content.text: Quality in a Claude integration is defined by four properties: the integration produces the outputs specified in the DO list, avoids the outputs prohibited in the DO NOT list, resists adversarial inputs that attempt to circumvent its constraints, and maintains these properties as the prompt and the model evolve over time. The quality scorecard tracks each property with a measurable metric.
% of golden dataset cases passing with the current prompt and model
Absolute drop in eval pass rate from the prior sprint
% of injection probes the integration resists (0 succeeds = 100%)
Number of golden dataset cases per capability item (DO/DO NOT)
% of eval cases that produce inconsistent results across re-runs
Days since the golden dataset was last reviewed and updated

The quality scorecard is most useful as a trend signal, not a point-in-time measurement. An integration that scores 88 three sprints in a row is healthy; one that scores 92, then 88, then 84 is on a trajectory that will hit the amber threshold in two more sprints. The sprint-over-sprint regression delta metric captures this trend directly, but the overall score trend is the richer signal.
no action required. Monitor for trend direction.
identify the lowest-scoring metric. Create a GitHub issue assigning a specific engineer to investigate root cause within the sprint.
Red (< 70) or any injection probe failure:
treat as a release blocker. The integration should not be promoted to production until the score recovers to amber or above.

Reliability measures whether users can successfully use the Claude integration when they try. A reliable integration has high availability, responds within the expected latency SLA, has a low error rate, and does not frequently require rollbacks. Reliability is measured over a rolling window (typically 7 or 30 days) rather than against a snapshot, because reliability is a property of a running system rather than a release artifact.
% of minutes with at least one successful response in the monitoring window
P95 latency as % of SLA (7-day rolling)
P95 response time divided by the declared latency SLA
% of requests that return a non-200 status or stop_reason of 'error'
% of production deployments that required a rollback within 7 days
Average time (hours) from incident detection to resolution
The Relationship Between Quality and Reliability
Quality and reliability failures have different causes and different remediation paths, but they interact:
A quality regression can cause reliability degradation:

if a prompt regression causes the model to produce very long outputs in edge cases, the P95 latency rises and the max_tokens truncation rate increases. The underlying cause is a quality issue (prompt regression), but it manifests as a reliability symptom (latency spike).
A reliability incident can mask quality:

if the integration has a high error rate, eval coverage becomes harder to interpret — failed evals due to API errors look the same as failed evals due to model behaviour issues. Stabilise reliability before drawing quality conclusions from an eval run taken during a high-error-rate period.
Both scorecards must be green for promotion:
the release gate requires both quality and reliability to be above threshold. A team with a green quality score but a red reliability score is not ready for production promotion.

Quality asks: given that the request reaches the model, does it respond correctly? Reliability asks: does the request reach the model at all, and does the response arrive in time? Both must hold for the integration to serve users well.
After each eval gate run (per PR and weekly batch)
Golden dataset snapshot at time of run
Amber or Red status, or > 3% sprint-on-sprint decline
Continuous (metrics update in real time); summarised weekly
7-day rolling for latency/errors, 30-day rolling for availability/rollbacks
Any metric entering amber; any P2+ incident in the window
Any dimension red; security posture amber or red

Reliability metrics for a Claude integration require instrumentation at three points: the API call boundary, the application response path, and the deployment pipeline. The minimum instrumentation set:

import time, structlog
log = structlog.get_logger()

async def call_claude_with_metrics(template, context: str, user_message: str):
    start = time.monotonic()
    error = None
    stop_reason = None

    try:
        response = await client.messages.create(
            model=template.model_id,
            max_tokens=template.max_output_tokens,
            system=template.system_prompt,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
        )
        stop_reason = response.stop_reason
    except Exception as e:
        error = type(e).__name__
        raise
    finally:
        duration_ms = (time.monotonic() - start) * 1000
        log.info(
            "claude_api_call_metrics",
            template_id=template.template_id,
            duration_ms=round(duration_ms, 1),
            stop_reason=stop_reason,
            error=error,
            # Reliability dashboard reads these fields:
            # - duration_ms → P95 latency computation
            # - stop_reason == "max_tokens" → truncation rate
            # - error is not None → error rate
        )

    return response
Using production error rate as the quality signal:

a low production error rate does not mean the integration is producing correct outputs — only that API calls are succeeding. Eval pass rate is the quality signal; error rate is the reliability signal.
Averaging quality and reliability into one score:

when quality and reliability are averaged, a high reliability score can compensate for a quality deficit. A system that is always up but always wrong should score as Red on quality and Green on reliability — not as Amber overall.
Setting latency SLA without measuring P95:

teams often define latency SLA based on the average or median response time, then monitor the median. For user experience, P95 matters: 95% of users receive the median experience, but 5% experience the tail. Monitor P95 against the SLA.

stop_reason=max_tokens is a reliability failure mode specific to LLM integrations. A high truncation rate means users are receiving incomplete responses. Standard API error rate monitoring will not catch this — it must be tracked as a separate signal.
Refreshing quality scorecard only on release:

eval pass rate can change between releases if the underlying model changes (Anthropic updates models without requiring integration changes). Run eval on a schedule (weekly at minimum) independent of the release cycle.
Full Scorecard Schema and Reporting Pipeline

Scorecard results are persisted as JSON so they can be trended over time and consumed by dashboards, Slack notifications, and the quarterly engineering review. Each weekly scorecard produces a file in the team's scorecard history directory:

{
  "scorecard_version": "1.0",
  "team": "invoice-assistant",
  "sprint": "2025-W26",
  "computed_at": "2025-06-30T09:00:00Z",

  "quality": {
    "score": 88.5,
    "status": "GREEN",
    "metrics": {
      "eval_pass_rate": 0.934,
      "eval_pass_rate_score": 100,
      "regression_delta": 0.012,
      "regression_delta_score": 100,
      "injection_pass_rate": 1.0,
      "injection_pass_rate_score": 100,
      "eval_coverage_per_capability": 7,
      "eval_coverage_score": 100,
      "flaky_rate": 0.04,
      "flaky_rate_score": 100,
      "dataset_staleness_days": 45,
      "dataset_staleness_score": 100
    }
  },

  "reliability": {
    "score": 79.2,
    "status": "AMBER",
    "metrics": {
      "availability_30d": 99.6,
      "availability_score": 100,
      "p95_latency_pct_of_sla": 0.82,
      "p95_latency_score": 70,
      "error_rate_pct": 1.8,
      "error_rate_score": 72,
      "max_tokens_truncation_pct": 3.1,
      "truncation_rate_score": 100,
      "rollback_rate_pct": 0,
      "rollback_rate_score": 100,
      "mttr_hours": 2.5,
      "mttr_score": 70
    }
  },

  "combined_status": "AMBER",
  "action_required": [
    "P95 latency at 82% of SLA — investigate context size growth",
    "Error rate at 1.8% — review API timeout configuration"
  ]
}

# .github/workflows/scorecard-report.yml
name: Weekly Scorecard Report

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 9 * * MON'   # Every Monday 09:00 UTC
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  scorecard:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - name: Collect quality metrics
        run: python scripts/collect-quality-metrics.py > quality-metrics.json

      - name: Collect reliability metrics
        run: python scripts/collect-reliability-metrics.py > reliability-metrics.json

      - name: Compute scorecard
        run: |
          python scripts/compute-scorecard.py \
            --quality quality-metrics.json \
            --reliability reliability-metrics.json \
            --output scorecard-$(date +%Y-W%V).json

      - name: Store scorecard history
        run: |
          mkdir -p .scorecard-history
          cp scorecard-$(date +%Y-W%V).json .scorecard-history/
          git config user.email "ci@example.com"
          git config user.name "CI Bot"
          git add .scorecard-history/
          git commit -m "scorecard: $(date +%Y-W%V)" || echo "No changes"
          git push

      - name: Post to Slack if amber or red
        run: |
          STATUS=$(python -c "import json; d=json.load(open('scorecard-$(date +%Y-W%V).json')); print(d['combined_status'])")
          if [ "$STATUS" != "GREEN" ]; then
            python scripts/post-scorecard-slack.py scorecard-$(date +%Y-W%V).json
          fi
        env:
          SLACK_WEBHOOK: ${{ secrets.SCORECARD_SLACK_WEBHOOK }}
Using the scorecard in sprint retrospectives

The quality and reliability scorecards provide concrete data for sprint retrospectives. Rather than relying on anecdote ('this sprint felt rough on production stability'), the team reviews the scorecard trend and identifies the specific metrics that drove score changes:
Open the scorecard history for the last three sprints side by side.
Identify metrics that changed by more than 10 points sprint-on-sprint.
For each large change, assign an owner to present: what caused the change, what was done in response, and whether the change has stabilised.
For amber or red metrics, create a GitHub issue with a specific target and owner before the retrospective ends.
Track whether prior-sprint action items actually moved the metric — if an action item was 'investigate P95 latency' but latency did not improve, escalate to the engineering manager.
Metrics that are hard to move
Some scorecard metrics are structural rather than addressable in a single sprint. Recognising them avoids setting unrealistic targets:

MTTR is bounded by how long Anthropic API incidents last. If the upstream API goes down for 2 hours, the integration's MTTR for that incident is at least 2 hours. Track MTTR split by cause (upstream vs own service) to distinguish what the team can actually improve.

rollbacks are rare events. In a sprint with no rollbacks, the rolling-window rollback rate does not change. In a sprint with one rollback, it spikes. Accept high variance on this metric and trend it over a quarter, not a sprint.

staleness grows linearly with time and drops sharply when a review is conducted. Teams that review the dataset every quarter will have a sawtooth pattern on this metric. Set a target of < 90 days and schedule a calendar event to review it, rather than treating each amber reading as a crisis.
The quality and reliability scorecards aggregate metrics that are also enforced as release gates. The relationship:
Team investigates root cause in current sprint
Immediate security team notification; no sprint-level delay
Investigate in next sprint if sustained
Investigate context size growth and timeout settings

The CI gate is a binary decision: the release passes or it does not. The scorecard is a trend signal: the integration is healthy, declining, or in need of attention. An integration can pass every CI gate while the scorecard trends downward — for example, if gates are barely clearing at minimum thresholds sprint after sprint. The scorecard catches sustained marginal quality that the gate's binary decision cannot.

Quality and reliability scorecards measure orthogonal properties of a Claude integration. Quality scorecards track correctness — whether the integration produces the right outputs, resists adversarial inputs, and maintains this behaviour over time. Reliability scorecards track stability — whether the integration is available, fast, and error-free. Both must be green for the integration to serve users well. Neither can substitute for the other.
Eval pass rate and injection probe resistance are the primary quality signals; availability and P95 latency are the primary reliability signals.
Quality is measured against the golden dataset at a point in time; reliability is measured as a rolling window over live traffic.
A quality regression can manifest as a reliability symptom (latency spike from long outputs); a reliability incident can distort quality measurements (errors look like eval failures).
Both scorecards feed into the team health scorecard, where they are weighted independently rather than averaged into a single quality-reliability number.
Minimum instrumentation for reliability requires logging duration_ms, stop_reason, and error on every API call — these three fields populate all six reliability scorecard metrics.
